coredata.dc:title: Exploring multi-element co-doped carbon dots as dual-mode probes for fluorescence/CT imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Peide
Zhao, Xuelin
Zhu, Qingyan
Han, Xinli
Tang, Yaoyao
Liao, Song
Guo, Zixuan
Wang, Ziying
Bi, Wenzhi
Xu, Quan
Zhang, Lunan
Xu, Meng
coredata.dc:description: Osteosarcoma (OS), the most common pediatric malignant bone tumor, has been stagnant in its current treatment status due to its highly heterogeneous, aggressive, and lethal nature. Herein, we developed multi-element charge-transfer co-doped carbon dots (CDs) as dual-mode contrast agents for OS models using hydrothermal carbonization. The obtained P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 have high long-wavelength quantum yields (16.5, 27.3, and 14.2%) in N, N-dimethylformamide solution, which can be attributed to the presence of valence electrons in the doped metal ions or metal hydrochloride, which promotes the radiative recombination of electrons and the formation of holes. Moreover, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 exhibited higher biocompatibility, good 143B cell imaging, and fluorescence/CT high contrast imaging performance in Balb/C tumor-bearing mice compared to P, W co-doped CDs, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2. In addition, the biodistribution and pharmacokinetic results further indicate that P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 has apparent advantages in imaging tracking and biosafety of OS and has excellent potential as a dual-mode imaging contrast agent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Osteosarcoma
Computed tomography/fluorescence imaging
Biosafety
Dual-modal contrast agent

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological characterization and analysis of three co-doped CDs. (A, E, I) HRTEM images, (B, F, J) XRD patterns, (C, G, K) AFM images, and (D, H, L) corresponding height analysis of P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2.

Structural characterization of three co-doped CDs. (A) TGA, (B) FT-IR, (C)XPS full scan, (D) relative content of different elements, high resolution of (E) C 1 s, (F) N 1 s, (G) O 1 s and (H) Hf 4f7/2, and (I) UV–vis spectra of P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2.

Optical properties and in vitro imaging of three co-doped CDs. Photoluminescence spectra of P, W co-doped CDs (A), P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 (C), and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 (E) at the excitation wavelength of 360 ∼ 540 nm. Confocal fluorescence images of 143B cells incubated with P, W co-doped CDs (B), P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 (D), and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 (F). Scale bar: 50 μm.

(A) In vivo fluorescence imaging of 143B tumor-bearing Balb/C mice before (0 h) and after injection of P, W co-doped CDs (a1), P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 (a2) and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 (a3) for 2, 6 and 12 h. Quantitative analysis of the average fluorescence signal and time of tumor sites in a1 (B), a2 (C) and a3 (D) groups of mice.

(A) In vitro CT images of different concentrations of iodixanol (a1), P, W co-doped CDs (a2), P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 (a3) and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 (a4). (B) Bone mineral density intensity of aqueous solutions of iodiol (a1), P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 (a3) and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 (a4) as a function of material concentration. (C) 3D X-ray CT imaging and transverse scanning images of Balb/C tumor-bearing mice after intravenous injection of P, W co-doped CDs (c1, c4), P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 (c2, c5) and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 (c3, c6). (D) Pharmacokinetics of Balb/C tumor-bearing mice after intravenous injection of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2. Biodistribution of Balb/C tumor-bearing mice after intravenous injection of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 (E) and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 (F).

In vivo systemic toxicity. (A) Body weight of healthy mice under saline, P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2. (B-F) Biochemical analysis of sera from different groups of mice (n = 3). (G) H&E staining of major organs of mice after 10 days of intravenous administration.
Schematic synthesis of P, W co-doped CDs, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs by solvothermal carbonization of ternary/quaternary mixtures (A) and following applications (B).

head.ce:title: Exploring multi-element co-doped carbon dots as dual-mode probes for fluorescence/CT imaging

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Osteosarcoma (OS), the most common pediatric malignant bone tumor, has been stagnant in its current treatment status due to its highly heterogeneous, aggressive, and lethal nature. Herein, we developed multi-element charge-transfer co-doped carbon dots (CDs) as dual-mode contrast agents for OS models using hydrothermal carbonization. The obtained P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 have high long-wavelength quantum yields (16.5, 27.3, and 14.2%) in N, N-dimethylformamide solution, which can be attributed to the presence of valence electrons in the doped metal ions or metal hydrochloride, which promotes the radiative recombination of electrons and the formation of holes. Moreover, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 exhibited higher biocompatibility, good 143B cell imaging, and fluorescence/CT high contrast imaging performance in Balb/C tumor-bearing mice compared to P, W co-doped CDs, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2. In addition, the biodistribution and pharmacokinetic results further indicate that P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 has apparent advantages in imaging tracking and biosafety of OS and has excellent potential as a dual-mode imaging contrast agent.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Osteosarcoma (OS), one of the most common malignancies in children and adolescents, severely impacts life safety due to its highly heterogeneous, aggressive, and lethal nature [1–3]. The standard therapeutic strategy for OS is neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by surgical resection of the primary tumor and postoperative chemotherapy [2,4]. Despite the outstanding efforts of researchers to improve the prognosis of patients with OS, the histological response to preoperative chemotherapy may take 2 to 3 months to assess [5,6], and this lengthy assessment procedure severely affects the last therapeutic adjustments for OS [7,8]. Clinically, OS is detected mainly by radiographs, computed tomography (CT), and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [9–11]. However, these methods have not yet achieved a simple, rapid, and convenient early diagnosis of OS. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a new multimodal collaborative early diagnosis method to improve the survival rate of OS patients.

Over the past decades, medical imaging has become one of the most effective techniques for disease diagnosis and surveillance [12–14]. Unfortunately, each imaging technique has shown different advantages in terms of resolution, screening for pathological changes, or sensitivity and no longer meets all the needs of medical diagnosis [15,16]. For example, fluorescence imaging (FI) has the sensitivity of single-cell imaging, but its clinical application has the disadvantages of poor temporal and spatial resolution [15–17]. CT, MRI, and other standard techniques are extremely sensitive to electron-dense elements and structural information and have broad coverage and excellent quantitative capabilities for detecting cardiovascular, neurological, congenital malformations, and other diseases. However, these techniques still need to be improved, for example, to improve sensitivity and accuracy further when screening for specific conditions [18,19]. In this context, advances in nanotechnology have facilitated the development of multifunctional nanoparticles. These nanoparticles integrate two or three imaging contrast agents that can be synergistically combined on the same nanoprobe to complement each other and improve diagnostic and therapeutic results [17,20], such as CuInS2/ZnS core/shell nanocrystals [21], NaYF4:Yb, Er@NaGdF4:Ce, Ln nanocrystals [22], and Tm co-doped NaYF4:Yb up-conversion nanoparticles, etc [23]. However, nanocrystals or up-conversion materials suffer from poor stability, low water solubility, and complicated synthesis processes in biological media, which are the main drawbacks of conventional multimodal nanoprobes used in clinical diagnosis. In contrast, carbon dots (CDs) have potential applications in imaging, multicolor patterning, sensing, cancer therapy, and even MR imaging after doping with Gd, Yb, or Mn ions due to their unique optical properties, low-cost preparation, good biocompatibility, and ease of surface functionalization and doping [24–26]. However, to our knowledge, CDs doped with P, W, and Hf exhibit dual-modal bioimaging performance in OS models, including the contrast ability for simultaneous fluorescence and CT imaging, which have never been reported yet.

In this work, we prepared three multi-element charge-transfer co-doped CDs, namely P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2, for the first time by solvothermal carbonization of ternary/quaternary mixtures. By studying the effects of metal ion doping on the fluorescence properties of CDs, we found that the presence of valence electrons in P, W, and Hf ions can promote electron transfer between metal ions and CDs, leading to the formation of radiative electron recombination and holes on the surface of CDs, enabling the materials to exhibit excellent physicochemical properties, biocompatibility, and in vitro imaging effects. By comparing the physicochemical properties, biodistribution, and pharmacokinetics of the three materials, we further found that P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 exhibited intense and stable dual-emission fluorescence, excellent QYs, and outstanding fluorescence/CT dual-mode imaging properties of OS models, which could be applied as dual-mode probes for clinical research exploration in OS patients.

Scheme 1 shows the synthesis and application of this work. O-phenylenediamine was used as the carbon source. Sodium phosphotungstate octadecahydrate was chosen as the feedstock for P and W doping. Acetic acid was used as the acid-base solution. N, N-dimethylformamide was selected as the solvent for deep nitrogen doping. The synthetic conditions of P, W co-doped CDs, including O-phenylenediamine (OPD), acetic acid, temperature, and time, are discussed in detail in the Supporting Information. As shown in Table S1, the content of graphite carbon increased with increasing OPD [27], leading to an increase and then a decrease in the QYs of the P, W co-doped CDs. Therefore, we chose the mass ratio of OPD to sodium phosphotungstate octadecahydrate of 6:1 as the optimal feedstock ratio to investigate the effect of acetic acid content on the QYs of P, W co-doped CDs. We found that the QYs of P, W co-doped CDs increased and then decreased with the increase of acetic acid content (Table S1). In addition, in the process of analyzing the effects of reaction time and temperature on the fluorescence QYs of P, W co-doped CDs, we also found that the QYs first increased and then decreased with the increase of reaction time or temperature (Table S1), which may be due to the excessive carbonization and dehydration of the precursor materials caused by too high temperature or long reaction time, resulting in partial or total loss of functional groups on the surface of CDs, thereby weakening or quenching their photoluminescence emission intensity [28]. On this basis, we also discussed the effect of Hf doping on the QYs of CDs. It can be seen from Table S1 that the QYs of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs first decrease, then increase, and finally decrease. Hence, we selected the optimal reaction conditions described above to develop P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 and discussed in detail the physicochemical properties, biodistribution, and their fluorescence and CT dual-mode imaging capabilities in the OS model for these three materials (Scheme 1B).

The prepared P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 were selected for further study as described previously. The morphology and size distribution of the CDs were obtained by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM). As shown in Fig. 1 A, E, and I, the sizes and lattice fringes of P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 are 3.1, 4.2, 6.8 nm and 0.21, 0.21, 0.21 nm, respectively (Fig. S1), corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphite carbon [29]. The AFM morphology heights of the three CDs are shown in Fig. 1C, 1G, 1 K, S2, and 1D, 1H, 1L, it can be seen that the average morphological heights of P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 are 2.8, 4.1 and 7.5 nm, respectively, and have good mono-dispersity [30]. To further investigate the crystallization properties of the CDs, we also performed XRD and Raman spectroscopy on the above three CDs. The XRD patterns of the three CDs are shown in Fig. 1B, 1F, and 1 J, with the strongest peaks near 18.3° and the smaller size of the carbon crystals compared to the typical graphitic carbon (26.5°) plane, which may be caused by the ordered decrease of the sp2 layer due to the highly defective carbon structure [31,32]. The Raman spectra of P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 show two distinct peaks at 1344 and 1586 cm−1 in Fig. S3, attributed to the D band (sp3 hybrid carbon) and G band (sp2 hybrid carbon) with ID/IG ratios of 1.82, 1.79, and 1.90, respectively, indicating that the three materials have good crystallinity [33,34].

The thermal stability analysis of P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 is shown in Fig. 2 A, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 are more stable than that of P, W co-doped CDs. The initial weight loss between 0 and 200 °C was 14%, 8%, and 4% for the three CDs, respectively, which may be due to the weak hydrogen bonding of water molecules and other substances on the nanoparticles [35]. And a steady weight loss is observed for the three CDs until 550 °C. Unlike P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 have relatively small weight loss of 88.3% and 71.0%, respectively may be attributed to the presence of Hf. Therefore, it can be inferred that the Hf content in P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 is 9.34% and 26.64%, respectively [36,37]. In addition, we also determined the Hf contents of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, Fig. S4), which were 7.45% and 28.2%, respectively, in general agreement with the above thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) results.

The three CDs’ chemical structure, elemental composition, and functional groups were investigated using Fourier infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray electron spectroscopy (XPS). The FT-IR spectra of the three CDs are shown in Fig. 2B, and all samples show similar absorption bands in the 3402 cm−1, which are identified as the stretching vibration of O-H and N-H; the small band between 2930 cm−1 is ascribed to the C-H group; the peak centered at 1618 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CO. In addition, three peaks at 1600, 1480, and 1400 cm−1 indicate the existence of CC in these three CDs; the typical peak at 750–1200 cm−1 is considered to be the doping of P and W elements [38,39]. Unlike the FT-IR spectra of the P, W co-doped CDs, the P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CD-2 exhibit a characteristic peak at 623 cm−1, which is caused by the vibration of Hf-O [40]. Moreover, we further investigated the structure and composition of these three materials by XPS. The XPS full scan spectra in Fig. 2C show that the three CDs are mainly composed of typical peaks of C, N, and O (284.5, 399.7, and 530.5 eV, respectively). In addition to the three elements, trace amounts of W and P are detected in the three CDs, which come from the starting material of sodium phosphotungstate. Hf is also detected in P, W, Hf co-doped CD-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2, which come from the starting material of hafnium chloride, and the relative contents of the different elements are shown in Fig. 2D. As shown in Fig. 2E, the high-resolution C 1s spectra have four prominent peaks at 283.9, 284.6, 285.2, and 286.1 eV, corresponding to the CC, C–C, C–N, and C–O bonds, respectively [41]. The spectral peaks of the high-resolution N 1s in Fig. 2F are at 398.7, 399.9, and 400.7 eV, indicating the presence of N mainly in pyridine N, pyrrole N, and graphite N. The content of graphite N is a crucial parameter causing a significant redshift of photoluminescence spectrum from 560 to 630 nm, which increases from 45.3% in the P, W co-doped CDs to 51.4% in the P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 to 30.6% in the P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 (Table S2) [41,42]. The high-resolution O 1 s spectra at 530, 531, and 532 eV can be divided into three parts associated with C–O, CO, and C–OH bands, respectively (Fig. 2G). Among them, the C 1s spectra of P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 show an increase in CC content from 15.8% to 16.5% to 18.7%, respectively (Table S2), which confirms that the content of the sp2 domain increases with the redshift of photoluminescence wavelength and is another essential parameter to demonstrate the redshift of photoluminescence [42,43]. The high-resolution P 2p spectra of the P, W co-doped CDs, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 located at 166.3 eV correspond to P = O (Fig. S5) [44]. However, P 2p spectra were not detected in P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 due to the low P element content. As shown in Fig. S6, the high-resolution W 4f spectra of the three CDs at 35.5 and 37.6 eV are closely related to W 4f7/2 and W 4f5/2, respectively [38]. In addition, Fig. 2H shows the high-resolution Hf 4f7/2 spectra of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 at 16.9 and 22 eV, corresponding to Hf-O and Ntv Ox (i.e., intrinsic oxide) [45].

The best optical properties of the three CDs can be evaluated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 2I, the UV–vis spectra of the three CDs have three bands at 280 ∼ 300 nm, 375 ∼ 385 nm, and 490 ∼ 500 nm. The former can be attributed to the formation of polyaromatic chromophores, which belongs to the π-π* transition of CC in the sp2 core. The latter two correspond to the n-π* transition of CO [46,47]. Moreover, as the Hf doping content increases, the short wavelength shifts from 296.7 to 288 to 285 nm, and the long wavelength shifts from 494 to 461 to 496 nm. Meanwhile, the absorptions of P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 gradually decrease, decreasing the sp2 structural domain in the nucleus [41,46]. As shown in Fig. 3 A, 3C, and 3E, the fluorescence emission of the three CDs depend on the excitation wavelength, with the emission peak red-shifted from 560 to 630 nm as the excitation wavelength increases from 360 nm to 540 nm, and this unique photoluminescence emission phenomenon may be caused by their presence of trapped states in the band gap. In this case, photoexcited electrons and/or holes can be trapped and compounded, leading to radioluminescence emission at higher wavelengths (Fig. S7) [48]. Interestingly, besides the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, another attractive long-wavelength emission spectrum of the three CDs was observed in the solvent. As shown in Fig. S8, the longer emission wavelengths of the three CDs located near 560 nm show a clear red shift with decreasing solvent polarity. The emission characteristics of the CDs reflect that each sample has not only different energy levels and sp2 domain but also is independent of the doping content of Hf elements. Metal dopants and carbonates, such as Zn, Ba, and Mn, can promote the radiative recombination of electrons and the formation of holes on the surface of CDs, further enhancing the electron transfer effect and ultimately leading to an increase in QYs [49,50]. Therefore, we measured the QYs of P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 in N, N-dimethylformamide solutions with an integrating sphere spectrometer, which was 16.5, 27.3, and 14.2%, respectively, indicating that the doping with certain Hf content contributes to the increase of QYs. In addition, we further investigated the optical properties of the CDs through the time-resolved fluorescence decay curves shown in Fig. S9. We summarized the three CDs’ fluorescence lifetimes, overall contributions, and perfect-fitting parameters of the three CDs (Table S3). The results show that the fluorescence lifetimes of P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 are 4.84, 4.87, and 4.81 ns, respectively, with χ2 values ranged from 1.155 to 1.421 to 1.353, indicating that the sp2 structural domain plays a crucial role in the radiative lifetime, independent of the doping content of Hf elements [41]. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of the three CDs remains stable in solutions with pH values ranging from 2 to 11, while it decreases in solutions with strong acids or higher pH values (Fig. S10). This pH characteristic of CDs may be related to the protonation and deprotonation of their surface functional groups [34]. In addition, we also investigated the fluorescence intensity changes of the CDs under UV light irradiation, sodium chloride contents, and antioxidant properties of hydrogen peroxide to evaluate their optical stability further. As shown in Figs. S11 and S12, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs remained almost constant with increasing UV irradiation time or sodium chloride concentration, indicating that the three CDs have good optical stability. However, a significant fluorescence quenching of the fluorescence intensity of the three CDs occurred after the addition of 3% content of hydrogen peroxide (Fig. S13), indicating that these three CDs have good fluorescence sensing to hydrogen peroxide [51,52].

Since P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 have adjustable photoluminescence and photostability. We also investigated the biocompatibility of the above three materials. First, the cytotoxicity of the three materials was evaluated with 143B cells. As shown in Fig. S14, compared with P, W co-doped CDs and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2, the survival rate of 143B cells incubated with P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 in the concentration range of 0 ∼ 500 mg/mL for 24 h exceeded 90%, indicating that P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 have relatively good biocompatibility. On this basis, we further evaluated the imaging ability of the three materials in 143B cells through confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). As shown in Fig. 3B, 3D, and 3F, P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 were treated with 143B cells for 4 h, respectively. The fluorescence of the three materials showed bright green and red colors under CLSM continuous wavelength test. Meanwhile, the fluorescence signal of 143B cells cultured with P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 did not decrease significantly at different times (4, 8, 12 h), indicating their suitability for long-term fluorescence imaging (Figs. S15, S16, and S17). Subsequently, we also used the IVIS Lumina XR imaging system to study the three materials’ in vivo fluorescence imaging effects. As shown in Fig. 4 A, after P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 were injected into mice through the tail vein, respectively, strong fluorescence signals were detected in the tumor site of Balb/C mice, and strong fluorescence signals were observed even after 12 h of injection. Fig. 4B, 4C, and 4D show the quantitative analysis of the average fluorescence signal of the three materials at different time points at the tumor site, indicating that P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 have good fluorescence imaging in vivo. In addition, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 have strong tomographic imaging potential for radiolabeling due to the presence of Hf elements.

It is generally known that Hf4+ ions have various characteristics (strong X-ray absorption coefficient and half-filled f-orbital) that make their complexes formed by chelation with different substances very suitable as contrast agents for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and computed tomography (CT) [53]. Here, we investigated the X-ray attenuation ability of different Hf-doped CDs using a commercial contrast agent (iodixanol) as a control. As shown in Fig. 5 A and 5B, the bone mineral density of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 (Fig. 5B-a4) was higher than that of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 (Fig. 5B-a3) and iodixanol (Fig. 5B-a1) at the same concentrations. However, the slope of the linear relationship between the bone mineral density values of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 (Fig. 5B-a3) and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 (Fig. 5B-a4) at different concentrations was lower than that of iodixanol (Fig. 5B-a1), indicating that P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 have good contrast properties and can be used as effective contrast agents for CT imaging. Therefore, we further investigated the potential use of P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 as contrast agents for in vivo CT imaging. Using Balb/C tumor-bearing mice as a model, CDs doped with different Hf elements were injected into the mice at a dose of 60 mg/kg through the tail vein. CT imaging showed a significant signal enhancement effect within the tumor (Fig. 5C). Compared with P, W co-doped CDs, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2, the signal of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 in the tumor was more decisive at 10 min after injection (Fig. 5C-c1, 5C-c2, and 5C-c3), indicating that P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 could be effectively enriched in the mouse tumor, these results were further confirmed in CT transverse scans at different levels (Fig. 5C-c4, 5C-c5, and 5C-c6). In addition, the residual amount of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 decays much slower in the blood than P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2, as shown in Fig. 5D, indicating that P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 has a longer circulation time. Since the prolonged cycle time increases drug enrichment in tumors through high permeability and retention (EPR) effect targeting in solid tumors, we subsequently performed biodistribution studies of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2. As shown in Fig. 5E and 5F, the drug enrichment of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 in tumor sites was significantly higher than that of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 and higher than other organs. These results suggest that P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 can be better enriched in tumor sites with outstanding fluorescence/CT imaging contrast performance.

Inspired by the excellent fluorescence/CT imaging performance of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, we explored the biosafety of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 in mice. Firstly, we injected saline, P, W co-doped CDs, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1, and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2 into mice through the tail vein four times at the prescribed doses and analyzed the effects of different drug treatments on the body weight of healthy mice. As shown in Fig. 6 A, there was no significant difference in the body weight of the mice in each group, indicating that CDs did not affect the health of the mice [18,45]. In addition, we injected different doses of the drug into the tail vein of healthy mice and then measured the physiochemical blood parameters of the mice with an instrument. As shown in Fig. 6B-6F, the blood biochemical parameters of alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), blood urea nitrogen (BUN), and serum creatinine (CRE) in mice treated with the above three materials fluctuated within the normal range with other mice, indicating the good biosafety of these three materials. Moreover, we also investigated the hemocompatibility of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 in the blood of mice by hemolysis assay. As shown in Fig. S18, the hemolysis rate of the three materials was less than 5% at concentrations up to 200 mg/mL, demonstrating the outstanding hemocompatibility of P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1. Finally, we performed a hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining analysis of the major organs of mice (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) on day 10. Fig. 6G shows no significant morphological changes in mice’s primary tissues and organs treated with P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 compared with the saline group, indicating that P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 has good low toxicity.

In conclusion, we have developed several multi-element charges transfer-doped CDs by hydrothermal carbonization method using O-phenylenediamine, sodium phosphotungstate octadecahydrate, and hafnium chloride as raw materials. They can be used as dual-mode fluorescent and CT imaging contrast agents due to their strong dual emission, high long-wavelength QYs, and excellent imaging properties. Therefore, we investigated the potential effects of different CDs as fluorescence and CT imaging contrast agents. The biodistribution and pharmacokinetic results showed that P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 not only ensure aggregation in the tumor region but also can be effectively retained compared to P, W co-doped CDs and P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-2, which have significant advantages in tumor imaging tracking and biosafety. In addition, the high atomic number of Hf gave the material a strong X-ray attenuation ability, enhancing its potential applications. Based on these advantages, P, W, Hf co-doped CDs-1 have great potential for application as dual-modal probes in biomedical research and disease diagnosis, providing a further clinical translation of cancer therapy.

Quan Xu and Meng Xu designed and directed the study. Peide Zhu and Xuelin Zhao performed experiments, wrote, and prepared the manuscript. Xinli Han, Yaoyao Tang, Song Liao, Zixuan Guo, and Ziying Wang provided materials and technical supports. Peide Zhu, Wenzhi Bi, Lunan Zhang: Mothodology, Writing - original draft. Qingyan Zhu and Peide Zhu: Writing - review & editing, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81972901), Beijing National Science Foundation (No. 3222018), National Defense Science and Technology Foundation Enhancement Plan (No. 2019-JCJQ-JJ-147), Key Basic Research Projects of the National Defense Science and Technology Foundation Enhancement Plan (No. 2019-JCJQ-ZD-120-40), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52211530034), Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum (No. 2462020YXZZ018).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.144042.
